main_paper.raw: {"methods":["Unsupervised Concept Bottleneck Models (UCBMs): converting pretrained black-box models into CBMs by discovering concepts via unsupervised concept discovery (using CRAFT)","Input-dependent concept selection mechanism: dynamically retaining a sparse set of relevant concepts per input using TopK activation and sparsity enforcement","Interpretable linear classifier trained on concept alignment scores","Editing model weights with guidance from large vision-language models to correct errors"],"problems":["Interpretability of deep neural networks (black-box models)","Dependence on costly human-annotated concepts in Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs)","Assumption of prior knowledge of learned concepts in post-hoc CBMs","Lack of input-dependent sparsity in concept usage, leading to reduced interpretability"],"datasets":[],"metrics":["Downstream classification performance (accuracy)","Sparsity (percentage of concepts used per input)","Interpretability (qualitative and user study)"],"results":[{"metric":"Sparsity (percentage of concepts used per input)","value":"as few as 1.4% of available concepts used per input (Table 2)"},{"metric":"Sparsity (number of concepts used per input)","value":"as few as 60 out of 4200 concepts used per input (Table 2)"},{"metric":"Downstream performance","value":"UCBMs improve performance compared to previous work (Tables 1 and 2)"}],"novelty_claims":["Propose Unsupervised Concept Bottleneck Models (UCBMs) that discover and use concepts learned by black-box models without requiring predefined or annotated concepts.","Introduce a novel input-dependent concept selection mechanism that dynamically retains a sparse set of relevant concepts for each input, greatly increasing sparsity and interpretability.","Demonstrate that UCBMs achieve higher sparsity and improved performance compared to previous CBM approaches.","Show that large vision-language models can guide weight editing in UCBMs to correct errors."]}

methods: Unsupervised Concept Bottleneck Models (UCBMs): converting pretrained black-box models into CBMs by discovering concepts via unsupervised concept discovery (using CRAFT)
Input-dependent concept selection mechanism: dynamically retaining a sparse set of relevant concepts per input using TopK activation and sparsity enforcement
Interpretable linear classifier trained on concept alignment scores
Editing model weights with guidance from large vision-language models to correct errors
problems: Interpretability of deep neural networks (black-box models)
Dependence on costly human-annotated concepts in Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs)
Assumption of prior knowledge of learned concepts in post-hoc CBMs
Lack of input-dependent sparsity in concept usage, leading to reduced interpretability
metrics: Sparsity (percentage of concepts used per input)

results.metric: Sparsity (percentage of concepts used per input)
results.value: as few as 1.4% of available concepts used per input (Table 2)

results.metric: Sparsity (number of concepts used per input)
results.value: as few as 60 out of 4200 concepts used per input (Table 2)
UCBMs improve performance compared to previous work (Tables 1 and 2)

novelty_claims: Propose Unsupervised Concept Bottleneck Models (UCBMs) that discover and use concepts learned by black-box models without requiring predefined or annotated concepts.
Introduce a novel input-dependent concept selection mechanism that dynamically retains a sparse set of relevant concepts for each input, greatly increasing sparsity and interpretability.
Demonstrate that UCBMs achieve higher sparsity and improved performance compared to previous CBM approaches.
Show that large vision-language models can guide weight editing in UCBMs to correct errors.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Language Guided Bottlenecks (LaBo): automatic construction of Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) using GPT-3 to generate candidate concepts and CLIP to align concepts to images","Novel submodular utility function for selecting discriminative and diverse concepts for the bottleneck layer","Prompting GPT-3 to generate factual, class-relevant sentences as candidate concepts","Using CLIP to score the presence of textual concepts in images and form the bottleneck layer"],"problems":["Manual specification of concepts in CBMs is costly and limits scalability","CBMs often underperform compared to black box models, limiting their adoption","Need for interpretable models that do not sacrifice accuracy compared to black box alternatives","Lack of methods for automatic, high-performance, interpretable-by-design classifiers without concept annotations"],"datasets":["11 diverse image classification datasets, including common objects (e.g., ImageNet, CIFAR), fine-grained types (e.g., CUB-200, FGVC-Aircraft, Stanford Cars, Oxford Flowers), textures (e.g., DTD), actions (e.g., Stanford 40 Actions), skin tumors (e.g., ISIC), and satellite images (e.g., EuroSAT)"],"metrics":["Classification accuracy (including few-shot accuracy, e.g., 1-shot)","Human evaluation of interpretability, factuality, and groundability"],"results":[{"metric":"1-shot classification accuracy improvement over linear probes","value":"11.7% higher"},{"metric":"Average classification accuracy over many dataset sizes compared to linear probes","value":"1.5% higher"},{"metric":"Performance compared to CBM modifications that circumvent bottlenecks","value":"Similar or better results without breaking CBM abstraction"}],"novelty_claims":["First to show how to construct high-performance CBMs without manual specification of concepts, achieving accuracy similar to black box models","Automatic construction of interpretable bottleneck layers using GPT-3 and CLIP, eliminating the need for expert-annotated concepts","Introduction of a novel submodular selection criterion for bottleneck concept selection, balancing interpretability and accuracy","Demonstration that interpretable models can match or exceed black box model performance across diverse tasks and data regimes","Human studies showing that GPT-3 generated bottlenecks are more factual and groundable than those from WordNet or Wikipedia"]}

methods: Language Guided Bottlenecks (LaBo): automatic construction of Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) using GPT-3 to generate candidate concepts and CLIP to align concepts to images
Novel submodular utility function for selecting discriminative and diverse concepts for the bottleneck layer
Prompting GPT-3 to generate factual, class-relevant sentences as candidate concepts
Using CLIP to score the presence of textual concepts in images and form the bottleneck layer
problems: Manual specification of concepts in CBMs is costly and limits scalability
CBMs often underperform compared to black box models, limiting their adoption
Need for interpretable models that do not sacrifice accuracy compared to black box alternatives
Lack of methods for automatic, high-performance, interpretable-by-design classifiers without concept annotations
datasets: 11 diverse image classification datasets, including common objects (e.g., ImageNet, CIFAR), fine-grained types (e.g., CUB-200, FGVC-Aircraft, Stanford Cars, Oxford Flowers), textures (e.g., DTD), actions (e.g., Stanford 40 Actions), skin tumors (e.g., ISIC), and satellite images (e.g., EuroSAT)
metrics: Human evaluation of interpretability, factuality, and groundability

results.metric: 1-shot classification accuracy improvement over linear probes
results.value: 11.7% higher

results.metric: Average classification accuracy over many dataset sizes compared to linear probes
results.value: 1.5% higher

results.metric: Performance compared to CBM modifications that circumvent bottlenecks
results.value: Similar or better results without breaking CBM abstraction

novelty_claims: First to show how to construct high-performance CBMs without manual specification of concepts, achieving accuracy similar to black box models
Automatic construction of interpretable bottleneck layers using GPT-3 and CLIP, eliminating the need for expert-annotated concepts
Introduction of a novel submodular selection criterion for bottleneck concept selection, balancing interpretability and accuracy
Demonstration that interpretable models can match or exceed black box model performance across diverse tasks and data regimes
Human studies showing that GPT-3 generated bottlenecks are more factual and groundable than those from WordNet or Wikipedia

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Label-free Concept Bottleneck Model (Label-free CBM): a framework to transform any neural network into an interpretable CBM without requiring labeled concept data, leveraging foundation models"],"problems":["The need for labeled data for each predefined concept in existing CBMs, which is time consuming and labor intensive","The lower accuracy of CBMs compared to standard neural networks, especially on complex datasets","The lack of scalable, efficient, and automated approaches for interpretable neural networks"],"datasets":["ImageNet"],"metrics":["Accuracy","User evaluation of interpretability"],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["Proposes the first CBM framework that does not require labeled concept data (label-free)","First CBM that scales to large datasets such as ImageNet","Efficient and automated process for creating CBMs, requiring minimal human effort and only a few hours even for large datasets","Transforms any neural network backbone into an interpretable CBM while retaining high accuracy comparable to the original neural network","Conducts large scale user evaluation of interpretability"]}

methods: Label-free Concept Bottleneck Model (Label-free CBM): a framework to transform any neural network into an interpretable CBM without requiring labeled concept data, leveraging foundation models
problems: The need for labeled data for each predefined concept in existing CBMs, which is time consuming and labor intensive
The lower accuracy of CBMs compared to standard neural networks, especially on complex datasets
The lack of scalable, efficient, and automated approaches for interpretable neural networks
novelty_claims: Proposes the first CBM framework that does not require labeled concept data (label-free)
First CBM that scales to large datasets such as ImageNet
Efficient and automated process for creating CBMs, requiring minimal human effort and only a few hours even for large datasets
Transforms any neural network backbone into an interpretable CBM while retaining high accuracy comparable to the original neural network
Conducts large scale user evaluation of interpretability

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Discover-then-Name Concept Bottleneck Model (DN-CBM): uses sparse autoencoders to discover concepts from CLIP feature extractors, automatically names concepts by matching dictionary vectors to text embeddings in CLIP space, and uses the named concept extractor as a bottleneck for classification","Automated concept extraction using sparse autoencoders (SAEs) on CLIP features","Automated concept naming by matching learned dictionary vectors to closest text embeddings in CLIP space","Task-agnostic concept discovery (concepts are discovered without reference to downstream tasks)"],"problems":["Lack of interpretability in deep neural networks ('black-box' problem)","Existing Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) require pre-specified, task-dependent concepts, which may not be detectable by the model","Faithfulness and semantic alignment of concepts in CBMs","Computational inefficiency and reliance on external LLMs for concept selection in CBMs"],"datasets":["CC3M (Concept discovery)","ImageNet","Places365","CIFAR10","CIFAR100"],"metrics":["Classification accuracy"],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["Proposes DN-CBM, a novel CBM that inverts the standard paradigm by discovering concepts first in a task-agnostic manner and then naming them, rather than pre-selecting concepts based on the task.","Introduces the use of sparse autoencoders (SAEs) to discover disentangled, human-interpretable concepts from CLIP feature extractors in vision tasks.","Presents a novel automated concept naming approach by aligning learned concept vectors with text embeddings in CLIP space, yielding semantically meaningful and interpretable concept names.","Demonstrates that the discovered and named concepts can be used as a frozen concept bottleneck for performant and interpretable classification across multiple downstream datasets, without the need for task-specific concept selection or LLM queries."]}

methods: Discover-then-Name Concept Bottleneck Model (DN-CBM): uses sparse autoencoders to discover concepts from CLIP feature extractors, automatically names concepts by matching dictionary vectors to text embeddings in CLIP space, and uses the named concept extractor as a bottleneck for classification
Automated concept extraction using sparse autoencoders (SAEs) on CLIP features
Automated concept naming by matching learned dictionary vectors to closest text embeddings in CLIP space
Task-agnostic concept discovery (concepts are discovered without reference to downstream tasks)
problems: Lack of interpretability in deep neural networks ('black-box' problem)
Existing Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) require pre-specified, task-dependent concepts, which may not be detectable by the model
Faithfulness and semantic alignment of concepts in CBMs
Computational inefficiency and reliance on external LLMs for concept selection in CBMs
novelty_claims: Proposes DN-CBM, a novel CBM that inverts the standard paradigm by discovering concepts first in a task-agnostic manner and then naming them, rather than pre-selecting concepts based on the task.
Introduces the use of sparse autoencoders (SAEs) to discover disentangled, human-interpretable concepts from CLIP feature extractors in vision tasks.
Presents a novel automated concept naming approach by aligning learned concept vectors with text embeddings in CLIP space, yielding semantically meaningful and interpretable concept names.

Demonstrates that the discovered and named concepts can be used as a frozen concept bottleneck for performant and interpretable classification across multiple downstream datasets, without the need for task-specific concept selection or LLM queries.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs): neural networks with an intermediate layer aligned to human-specified concepts, trained with an additional loss to predict concepts before predicting the final label","Intervention on concept predictions at test time: manually correcting predicted concepts to improve final prediction","Variants of training CBMs: different ways of training the concept prediction and label prediction stages"],"problems":["Lack of interpretability and manipulability in standard end-to-end neural networks, especially in high-stakes domains like medical diagnosis","Inability to interact with models using high-level concepts (e.g., 'bone spurs' in x-rays)","Tradeoff between predictive accuracy and interpretability in terms of human concepts","Difficulty in providing counterfactual explanations and supporting human-model collaboration"],"datasets":["Osteoarthritis grading dataset (Nevitt et al., 2006)","Caltech-UCSD Birds-200-2011 (CUB-200-2011) dataset (Wah et al., 2011)"],"metrics":["Task accuracy (final label prediction accuracy)","Concept accuracy (accuracy of predicted concepts)"],"results":[{"metric":"Task accuracy (CBM vs. end-to-end)","value":"CBMs achieve competitive or even higher accuracy compared to standard end-to-end models on both osteoarthritis grading and bird identification tasks"},{"metric":"Concept accuracy (CBM)","value":"CBMs attain high concept accuracy, enabling meaningful interventions"},{"metric":"Task accuracy after intervention","value":"Accuracy improves substantially beyond that of a standard model when concept mistakes are corrected at test time"}],"novelty_claims":["Propose a straightforward method to turn any end-to-end neural network into a concept bottleneck model using concept annotations at training time","Demonstrate that concept bottleneck models can achieve competitive accuracy with standard models while enabling direct human intervention and interpretability in terms of high-level concepts","Show that interventions on predicted concepts at test time can substantially improve model accuracy, supporting richer human-model interaction","Systematically study the trade-offs between task accuracy, concept accuracy, and the effect of interventions, including robustness to covariate shift"]}

methods: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs): neural networks with an intermediate layer aligned to human-specified concepts, trained with an additional loss to predict concepts before predicting the final label
Intervention on concept predictions at test time: manually correcting predicted concepts to improve final prediction
Variants of training CBMs: different ways of training the concept prediction and label prediction stages
problems: Lack of interpretability and manipulability in standard end-to-end neural networks, especially in high-stakes domains like medical diagnosis
Inability to interact with models using high-level concepts (e.g., 'bone spurs' in x-rays)
Tradeoff between predictive accuracy and interpretability in terms of human concepts
Difficulty in providing counterfactual explanations and supporting human-model collaboration

results.value: CBMs achieve competitive or even higher accuracy compared to standard end-to-end models on both osteoarthritis grading and bird identification tasks
CBMs attain high concept accuracy, enabling meaningful interventions
Accuracy improves substantially beyond that of a standard model when concept mistakes are corrected at test time

novelty_claims: Propose a straightforward method to turn any end-to-end neural network into a concept bottleneck model using concept annotations at training time
Demonstrate that concept bottleneck models can achieve competitive accuracy with standard models while enabling direct human intervention and interpretability in terms of high-level concepts
Show that interventions on predicted concepts at test time can substantially improve model accuracy, supporting richer human-model interaction
Systematically study the trade-offs between task accuracy, concept accuracy, and the effect of interventions, including robustness to covariate shift

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Post-hoc Concept Bottleneck Model (PCBM)","Turning any pre-trained neural network into a PCBM","Transferring concepts from other datasets or from natural language descriptions via multimodal models","Residual modeling step to recover black-box model performance","Global model editing via concept-level feedback"],"problems":["CBMs require dense concept annotations in training data, which are rarely available in real-world datasets","CBMs often do not match the accuracy of unrestricted neural networks","CBMs lack mechanisms for efficient global model editing to address spurious correlations or improve generalization","Difficulty in editing models holistically without retraining or access to target domain data"],"datasets":["CUB (Caltech-UCSD Birds)"],"metrics":["Model accuracy","Performance gains from model editing (user study)"],"results":[{"metric":"Model accuracy","value":"PCBMs achieve comparable performance to black-box models"},{"metric":"Performance gain from model editing","value":"Significant improvement via concept-level feedback without target domain data or retraining (as shown in user study)"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of Post-hoc Concept Bottleneck Models (PCBMs) that can convert any pre-trained model into a concept bottleneck model without requiring concept annotations in the training data","Ability to leverage concepts from other datasets or natural language descriptions via multimodal models, removing the need for laborious concept annotation","Introduction of a residual modeling step to recover original model performance when concepts are insufficient","Enabling efficient global model edits via concept-level feedback, as opposed to prior work focusing only on local interventions","Demonstration that PCBMs can be edited to improve performance without retraining or access to target domain data"]}

methods: Post-hoc Concept Bottleneck Model (PCBM)
Turning any pre-trained neural network into a PCBM
Transferring concepts from other datasets or from natural language descriptions via multimodal models
Residual modeling step to recover black-box model performance
Global model editing via concept-level feedback
problems: CBMs require dense concept annotations in training data, which are rarely available in real-world datasets
CBMs often do not match the accuracy of unrestricted neural networks
CBMs lack mechanisms for efficient global model editing to address spurious correlations or improve generalization
Difficulty in editing models holistically without retraining or access to target domain data
metrics: Performance gains from model editing (user study)

results.value: PCBMs achieve comparable performance to black-box models
Significant improvement via concept-level feedback without target domain data or retraining (as shown in user study)

novelty_claims: Introduction of Post-hoc Concept Bottleneck Models (PCBMs) that can convert any pre-trained model into a concept bottleneck model without requiring concept annotations in the training data
Ability to leverage concepts from other datasets or natural language descriptions via multimodal models, removing the need for laborious concept annotation
Introduction of a residual modeling step to recover original model performance when concepts are insufficient
Enabling efficient global model edits via concept-level feedback, as opposed to prior work focusing only on local interventions
Demonstration that PCBMs can be edited to improve performance without retraining or access to target domain data

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Instance-specific concept-based interventions on pretrained black-box neural networks by editing activations at an intermediate layer using a small validation set with concept labels","Formalization of 'intervenability' as a measure of intervention effectiveness","Fine-tuning black-box models using intervenability as a loss to improve intervention effectiveness while preserving model architecture and representations","Application of the above methods to models with concept labels acquired via vision-language models (VLM)"],"problems":["How to perform concept-based interventions on pretrained black-box neural networks that are not interpretable by design","How to improve the effectiveness of concept-based interventions on black-box models","How to enable instance-specific interventions in high-stakes domains (e.g., healthcare) without requiring large amounts of concept-labeled data","How to make black-box models more intervenable than traditional concept bottleneck models (CBMs)"],"datasets":["Synthetic tabular data","Natural image benchmarks","Deep chest X-ray (medical imaging) datasets"],"metrics":["Intervenability (effectiveness of concept-based interventions)","Predictive performance (e.g., classification accuracy)","Calibration of predictions"],"results":[{"metric":"Intervenability","value":"Fine-tuned black-box models show improved intervention effectiveness compared to both their untuned counterparts and CBMs"},{"metric":"Predictive performance","value":"Predictive performance of black-box models is improved via concept-based interventions"},{"metric":"Calibration","value":"Fine-tuned models often yield better-calibrated predictions"}],"novelty_claims":["First method to enable concept-based instance-specific interventions on pretrained black-box neural networks using only a small validation set with concept labels, without requiring changes to the model architecture or large-scale concept annotation.","Formalization of 'intervenability' as a quantitative measure and its use as a loss for fine-tuning black-box models to improve intervention effectiveness.","Demonstration that fine-tuned black-box models can be more intervenable than concept bottleneck models (CBMs).","Demonstration that the method works with concept labels acquired from vision-language models, reducing the need for human-annotated validation sets."]}

methods: Instance-specific concept-based interventions on pretrained black-box neural networks by editing activations at an intermediate layer using a small validation set with concept labels
Formalization of 'intervenability' as a measure of intervention effectiveness
Fine-tuning black-box models using intervenability as a loss to improve intervention effectiveness while preserving model architecture and representations
Application of the above methods to models with concept labels acquired via vision-language models (VLM)
problems: How to perform concept-based interventions on pretrained black-box neural networks that are not interpretable by design
How to improve the effectiveness of concept-based interventions on black-box models
How to enable instance-specific interventions in high-stakes domains (e.g., healthcare) without requiring large amounts of concept-labeled data
How to make black-box models more intervenable than traditional concept bottleneck models (CBMs)

results.value: Fine-tuned black-box models show improved intervention effectiveness compared to both their untuned counterparts and CBMs
Predictive performance of black-box models is improved via concept-based interventions
Fine-tuned models often yield better-calibrated predictions

novelty_claims: First method to enable concept-based instance-specific interventions on pretrained black-box neural networks using only a small validation set with concept labels, without requiring changes to the model architecture or large-scale concept annotation.
Formalization of 'intervenability' as a quantitative measure and its use as a loss for fine-tuning black-box models to improve intervention effectiveness.
Demonstration that fine-tuned black-box models can be more intervenable than concept bottleneck models (CBMs).
Demonstration that the method works with concept labels acquired from vision-language models, reducing the need for human-annotated validation sets.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Analysis of information leakage in Concept Learning Models (CLMs) with soft concept representations","Evaluation of mitigation strategies: task-blind training, adding unsupervised concept dimensions, and concept whitening","Demonstration of when and how information leakage occurs in CLMs","Suggestion of strategies to mitigate the effect of information leakage"],"problems":["Information leakage in concept representations of Concept Learning Models (CLMs)","Misleading interpretability of downstream predictions due to extra information encoded in concept representations","Ineffectiveness of existing mitigation strategies (task-blind training, concept whitening, unsupervised concept dimensions) in fully addressing information leakage"],"datasets":[],"metrics":[],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["Demonstrates that information leakage in CLMs is more pervasive and consequential than previously indicated in the literature","Shows that natural mitigation strategies (task-blind training, concept whitening, unsupervised concept dimensions) do not fully address information leakage in soft concept representations","Provides a detailed analysis of when and how information leakage occurs in CLMs with soft concept representations","Suggests new strategies to mitigate the effects of information leakage in CLMs"]}

methods: Analysis of information leakage in Concept Learning Models (CLMs) with soft concept representations
Evaluation of mitigation strategies: task-blind training, adding unsupervised concept dimensions, and concept whitening
Demonstration of when and how information leakage occurs in CLMs
Suggestion of strategies to mitigate the effect of information leakage
problems: Information leakage in concept representations of Concept Learning Models (CLMs)
Misleading interpretability of downstream predictions due to extra information encoded in concept representations
Ineffectiveness of existing mitigation strategies (task-blind training, concept whitening, unsupervised concept dimensions) in fully addressing information leakage
novelty_claims: Demonstrates that information leakage in CLMs is more pervasive and consequential than previously indicated in the literature
Shows that natural mitigation strategies (task-blind training, concept whitening, unsupervised concept dimensions) do not fully address information leakage in soft concept representations
Provides a detailed analysis of when and how information leakage occurs in CLMs with soft concept representations
Suggests new strategies to mitigate the effects of information leakage in CLMs

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Classification by description: using vision-language models (VLMs) to classify images based on descriptive features (descriptors) rather than just category names","Querying large language models (LLMs) such as GPT-3 to automatically generate visual descriptors for each class","Using descriptors as an interpretable bottleneck for classification, providing explanations for model decisions"],"problems":["Standard zero-shot classification with VLMs neglects rich descriptive information and lacks interpretability","Lack of intermediate understanding or explanation in VLM-based classification decisions","Difficulty in adjusting or specifying the criteria used for classification in VLMs","Scalability issues in hand-writing visual descriptors for large numbers of classes"],"datasets":["ImageNet"],"metrics":["Top-1 accuracy"],"results":[{"metric":"Top-1 ImageNet accuracy (improvement)","value":"~4-5% increase over baseline"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a new framework for classification with VLMs based on descriptive features (classification by description) rather than just category names","Introduces the use of large language models (LLMs) to automatically generate visual descriptors for categories, enabling scalability","Provides inherent interpretability and explainability in classification decisions by basing them on explicit features","Demonstrates improved accuracy across distribution shifts and the ability to adapt to unseen concepts without additional training or computational overhead","Shows that editing descriptors can effectively mitigate bias compared to the baseline"]}

methods: Classification by description: using vision-language models (VLMs) to classify images based on descriptive features (descriptors) rather than just category names
Querying large language models (LLMs) such as GPT-3 to automatically generate visual descriptors for each class
Using descriptors as an interpretable bottleneck for classification, providing explanations for model decisions
problems: Standard zero-shot classification with VLMs neglects rich descriptive information and lacks interpretability
Lack of intermediate understanding or explanation in VLM-based classification decisions
Difficulty in adjusting or specifying the criteria used for classification in VLMs
Scalability issues in hand-writing visual descriptors for large numbers of classes

results.value: ~4-5% increase over baseline

novelty_claims: Proposes a new framework for classification with VLMs based on descriptive features (classification by description) rather than just category names
Introduces the use of large language models (LLMs) to automatically generate visual descriptors for categories, enabling scalability
Provides inherent interpretability and explainability in classification decisions by basing them on explicit features
Demonstrates improved accuracy across distribution shifts and the ability to adapt to unseen concepts without additional training or computational overhead
Shows that editing descriptors can effectively mitigate bias compared to the baseline

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Causal mediation analysis to trace hidden state activations in GPT","Rank-One Model Editing (ROME) to directly edit feed-forward layer weights"],"problems":["Understanding where and how factual associations are stored in GPT-like autoregressive transformer models","Developing methods to locate and edit specific factual associations in large language models","Evaluating the specificity and generalization of model editing methods"],"datasets":["Standard zero-shot relation extraction (zsRE) benchmark","Newly introduced dataset of counterfactual assertions"],"metrics":["Model editing success rate on zsRE","Specificity of factual recall after editing","Generalization to counterfactual assertions"],"results":[{"metric":"Model editing success rate on zsRE","value":"ROME is similarly effective to other model editing approaches"},{"metric":"Specificity and generalization on counterfactual assertions","value":"ROME maintains both specificity and generalization, whereas other methods sacrifice one or the other"}],"novelty_claims":["First evidence that factual associations in GPT correspond to localized, directly-editable computations in mid-layer feed-forward modules","Introduction of Rank-One Model Editing (ROME) for directly editing factual associations in GPT","Demonstration that ROME achieves both specificity and generalization on a new counterfactual assertions dataset, outperforming previous methods that trade off one for the other","Introduction of a new dataset of counterfactual assertions for sensitive evaluation of model editing methods"]}

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Compositional Concept Extraction (CCE): a novel unsupervised method for extracting compositional concept representations by searching for entire subspaces of concepts at once, enforcing orthogonality and composability properties"],"problems":["Existing unsupervised concept extraction methods (e.g., PCA, KMeans) find concepts that are not compositional, limiting the interpretability and utility of concept-based explanations in foundation models","Lack of direct evaluation and extraction of compositional concept representations in current methods"],"datasets":["CUB (Caltech-UCSD Birds) dataset","Five different datasets over image and text data (exact names not all specified in the excerpt)"],"metrics":["Compositionality of extracted concepts (measured via compositionality evaluation protocols)","Downstream classification accuracy"],"results":[{"metric":"Compositionality of extracted concepts","value":"CCE finds more compositional concept representations than baselines (e.g., PCA, KMeans)"},{"metric":"Downstream classification accuracy","value":"CCE yields better accuracy on four downstream classification tasks compared to baselines"}],"novelty_claims":["First to directly address and evaluate the compositionality of concept representations extracted from foundation models","Identification and formalization of two salient properties (orthogonality and composability) necessary for compositional concept representations","Proposal of Compositional Concept Extraction (CCE), a novel method that enforces these properties by construction and extracts more compositional concepts than existing unsupervised methods","Demonstration that CCE improves downstream classification performance and interpretability over both vision and language datasets"]}

methods: Compositional Concept Extraction (CCE): a novel unsupervised method for extracting compositional concept representations by searching for entire subspaces of concepts at once, enforcing orthogonality and composability properties
problems: Existing unsupervised concept extraction methods (e.g., PCA, KMeans) find concepts that are not compositional, limiting the interpretability and utility of concept-based explanations in foundation models

novelty_claims: First to directly address and evaluate the compositionality of concept representations extracted from foundation models

Demonstration that CCE improves downstream classification performance and interpretability over both vision and language datasets

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["SegDiscover framework: (1) Generating concept primitives from raw images via unsupervised segmentation into patches/superpixels; (2) Discovering concepts by clustering these primitives in the latent space of a self-supervised pretrained encoder; (3) Concept refinement via neural network smoothing, where a segmentation network is trained to predict pseudo labels mapped from cluster assignments."],"problems":["Unsupervised discovery of semantically meaningful visual concepts from complex natural images.","Overcoming human bias and labor-intensiveness in concept definition by avoiding reliance on labeled data.","Identifying multiple concepts within a single image, including both foreground and background elements.","Improving interpretability of neural networks by providing a basis of visual concepts."],"datasets":["Cityscapes","COCO-Stuff"],"metrics":["Unsupervised semantic segmentation accuracy (e.g., clustering-based segmentation metrics, though specific metrics are not named in the provided text)"],"results":[{"metric":"Unsupervised semantic segmentation performance (Cityscapes and COCO-Stuff)","value":"SegDiscover outperforms state-of-the-art unsupervised methods on both datasets (exact numbers not provided in the text)."}],"novelty_claims":["Reframes visual concept discovery as an unsupervised semantic segmentation problem, enabling the discovery of multiple concepts per image without supervision.","Proposes a three-stage framework (concept primitive generation, latent space clustering, neural network smoothing) that directly embodies assumptions about natural image composition, local similarity, and global context.","Discovers concepts without any human-provided labels, reducing bias and manual effort.","Demonstrates the ability to discover coherent and meaningful concepts in complex real-world scenes, outperforming previous unsupervised methods.","Provides a tool for neural network explanation by visualizing the latent space of arbitrary pretrained encoders."]}

methods: SegDiscover framework: (1) Generating concept primitives from raw images via unsupervised segmentation into patches/superpixels; (2) Discovering concepts by clustering these primitives in the latent space of a self-supervised pretrained encoder; (3) Concept refinement via neural network smoothing, where a segmentation network is trained to predict pseudo labels mapped from cluster assignments.
problems: Unsupervised discovery of semantically meaningful visual concepts from complex natural images.
Overcoming human bias and labor-intensiveness in concept definition by avoiding reliance on labeled data.
Identifying multiple concepts within a single image, including both foreground and background elements.
Improving interpretability of neural networks by providing a basis of visual concepts.
metrics: Unsupervised semantic segmentation accuracy (e.g., clustering-based segmentation metrics, though specific metrics are not named in the provided text)

results.metric: Unsupervised semantic segmentation performance (Cityscapes and COCO-Stuff)
results.value: SegDiscover outperforms state-of-the-art unsupervised methods on both datasets (exact numbers not provided in the text).

novelty_claims: Reframes visual concept discovery as an unsupervised semantic segmentation problem, enabling the discovery of multiple concepts per image without supervision.

Proposes a three-stage framework (concept primitive generation, latent space clustering, neural network smoothing) that directly embodies assumptions about natural image composition, local similarity, and global context.
Discovers concepts without any human-provided labels, reducing bias and manual effort.
Demonstrates the ability to discover coherent and meaningful concepts in complex real-world scenes, outperforming previous unsupervised methods.
Provides a tool for neural network explanation by visualizing the latent space of arbitrary pretrained encoders.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Leveraging k-sparse autoencoders (k-SAEs) to identify and manipulate monosemantic concepts in the latent space of text-to-image diffusion models","Training k-SAEs on text prompt embeddings to isolate interpretable concepts","Using the trained k-SAE as a 'Concept Steerer' to control the presence or absence of specific concepts (e.g., nudity, violence, photographic style) during image generation without retraining or LoRA adapters"],"problems":["Unsafe, unethical, or adversarial content generation in text-to-image generative models","Computational inefficiency and quality degradation in existing concept removal or control methods","Lack of scalable, interpretable, and precise control over specific concepts in generative models"],"datasets":["Corpus of text prompts containing semantic concepts to be controlled (e.g., unsafe concepts)"],"metrics":["Unsafe concept removal rate","Generation quality (visual quality)","Inference speed (generation time)","Robustness to adversarial prompt manipulations"],"results":[{"metric":"Unsafe concept removal improvement","value":"20.01%"},{"metric":"Inference speedup over SOTA","value":"~5x faster"},{"metric":"Visual quality","value":"No compromise compared to baseline"}],"novelty_claims":["First to leverage k-sparse autoencoders to identify and manipulate monosemantic concepts in the latent space of text-to-image diffusion models for controllable generation","Achieves state-of-the-art unsafe concept removal while being ~5x faster than previous best methods, without compromising visual quality","Requires no retraining of the base model or LoRA adapters, making it highly efficient and adaptable","Demonstrates robustness to adversarial prompt manipulations and effective style/attribute manipulation","Provides a simple, interpretable, and generalizable framework for concept-level control in generative models"]}

methods: Leveraging k-sparse autoencoders (k-SAEs) to identify and manipulate monosemantic concepts in the latent space of text-to-image diffusion models
Training k-SAEs on text prompt embeddings to isolate interpretable concepts

Using the trained k-SAE as a 'Concept Steerer' to control the presence or absence of specific concepts (e.g., nudity, violence, photographic style) during image generation without retraining or LoRA adapters
problems: Unsafe, unethical, or adversarial content generation in text-to-image generative models
Computational inefficiency and quality degradation in existing concept removal or control methods
Lack of scalable, interpretable, and precise control over specific concepts in generative models
datasets: Corpus of text prompts containing semantic concepts to be controlled (e.g., unsafe concepts)

results.value: ~5x faster
No compromise compared to baseline

novelty_claims: First to leverage k-sparse autoencoders to identify and manipulate monosemantic concepts in the latent space of text-to-image diffusion models for controllable generation
Achieves state-of-the-art unsafe concept removal while being ~5x faster than previous best methods, without compromising visual quality
Requires no retraining of the base model or LoRA adapters, making it highly efficient and adaptable
Demonstrates robustness to adversarial prompt manipulations and effective style/attribute manipulation
Provides a simple, interpretable, and generalizable framework for concept-level control in generative models

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training (CLIP): Jointly training an image encoder and a text encoder to predict correct (image, text) pairings using a contrastive objective on a dataset of 400 million (image, text) pairs.","Zero-shot transfer: Using the learned text encoder to synthesize a zero-shot linear classifier by embedding the names or descriptions of target dataset classes."],"problems":["Limited generality and usability of computer vision systems trained on fixed, predetermined object categories requiring labeled data for new concepts.","Inefficiency and inflexibility of current supervised and weakly supervised pre-training approaches in transferring to new tasks or classes without retraining.","Lack of scalable methods for learning visual representations directly from natural language supervision at large scale."],"datasets":["400 million (image, text) pairs collected from the internet (proprietary dataset for pre-training)","Over 30 existing computer vision datasets for evaluation, including but not limited to: ImageNet, OCR datasets, action recognition datasets, geo-localization datasets, fine-grained object classification datasets"],"metrics":["Zero-shot classification accuracy","Transfer learning performance (accuracy on downstream tasks)","Linear-probe representation learning analysis","Computational efficiency (training speed, resource usage)","Robustness (comparison to supervised models)"],"results":[{"metric":"Zero-shot ImageNet accuracy (CLIP)","value":"Matches the accuracy of the original ResNet-50 on ImageNet without using any of the 1.28 million training examples"},{"metric":"Zero-shot ImageNet accuracy (Li et al., 2017)","value":"11.5%"},{"metric":"Zero-shot ImageNet accuracy (state of the art, Xie et al., 2020)","value":"88.4%"},{"metric":"Zero-shot ImageNet accuracy (classic computer vision, Deng et al., 2012)","value":"50%"},{"metric":"Zero-shot transfer efficiency (CLIP vs. image caption baseline)","value":"CLIP is 4x more efficient than bag-of-words baseline; transformer-based language models are 3x slower than BoW baseline"},{"metric":"Transfer performance","value":"CLIP is competitive with prior task-specific supervised models on over 30 datasets"}],"novelty_claims":["Demonstrates that large-scale contrastive pre-training on (image, text) pairs from the internet enables learning of highly transferable visual representations from natural language supervision.","Shows that CLIP can perform zero-shot transfer to a wide range of vision tasks, often matching or exceeding the performance of fully supervised baselines without any dataset-specific training.","Establishes that zero-shot CLIP models are more robust than supervised ImageNet models of equivalent accuracy.","Releases code and pre-trained model weights, enabling reproducibility and further research."]}

methods: Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training (CLIP): Jointly training an image encoder and a text encoder to predict correct (image, text) pairings using a contrastive objective on a dataset of 400 million (image, text) pairs.
Zero-shot transfer: Using the learned text encoder to synthesize a zero-shot linear classifier by embedding the names or descriptions of target dataset classes.
problems: Limited generality and usability of computer vision systems trained on fixed, predetermined object categories requiring labeled data for new concepts.
Inefficiency and inflexibility of current supervised and weakly supervised pre-training approaches in transferring to new tasks or classes without retraining.
Lack of scalable methods for learning visual representations directly from natural language supervision at large scale.
datasets: 400 million (image, text) pairs collected from the internet (proprietary dataset for pre-training)

Over 30 existing computer vision datasets for evaluation, including but not limited to: ImageNet, OCR datasets, action recognition datasets, geo-localization datasets, fine-grained object classification datasets
metrics: Transfer learning performance (accuracy on downstream tasks)

results.value: Matches the accuracy of the original ResNet-50 on ImageNet without using any of the 1.28 million training examples

results.metric: Zero-shot ImageNet accuracy (state of the art, Xie et al., 2020)
Zero-shot ImageNet accuracy (classic computer vision, Deng et al., 2012)
Zero-shot transfer efficiency (CLIP vs. image caption baseline)
results.value: CLIP is 4x more efficient than bag-of-words baseline; transformer-based language models are 3x slower than BoW baseline
CLIP is competitive with prior task-specific supervised models on over 30 datasets

novelty_claims: Demonstrates that large-scale contrastive pre-training on (image, text) pairs from the internet enables learning of highly transferable visual representations from natural language supervision.
Shows that CLIP can perform zero-shot transfer to a wide range of vision tasks, often matching or exceeding the performance of fully supervised baselines without any dataset-specific training.
Establishes that zero-shot CLIP models are more robust than supervised ImageNet models of equivalent accuracy.
Releases code and pre-trained model weights, enabling reproducibility and further research.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Training a very large autoregressive language model (GPT-3) with 175 billion parameters","Applying the model to tasks in a few-shot setting without any gradient updates or fine-tuning, using only text-based prompts and demonstrations"],"problems":["Improving few-shot learning in language models","Reducing the need for task-specific fine-tuning and large labeled datasets","Achieving strong performance on a wide range of NLP tasks with minimal task-specific adaptation"],"datasets":["Translation datasets (e.g., WMT)","Question-answering datasets (e.g., TriviaQA, WebQuestions)","Cloze tasks (e.g., LAMBADA)","Word unscrambling tasks","Novel word usage tasks","Arithmetic tasks (e.g., 3-digit arithmetic)","News article generation datasets"],"metrics":["Accuracy","F1 score","Human evaluation (for news article generation)","Perplexity"],"results":[{"metric":"Number of parameters (GPT-3)","value":"175 billion"},{"metric":"Few-shot performance on LAMBADA (accuracy)","value":"76.2%"},{"metric":"Few-shot performance on TriviaQA (accuracy)","value":"64.3%"},{"metric":"Human indistinguishability of news articles (human evaluation)","value":"Humans have difficulty distinguishing GPT-3 generated articles from human-written ones"}],"novelty_claims":["Demonstrates that scaling up language models to unprecedented size (175B parameters) enables strong few-shot learning without fine-tuning","Shows that a single large language model can perform competitively on a wide range of NLP tasks using only text-based prompts and a few examples","Establishes that few-shot learning performance improves substantially with model scale, sometimes rivaling state-of-the-art fine-tuned models","Reveals that GPT-3 can generate highly realistic text, including news articles that are difficult for humans to distinguish from real ones"]}

methods: Training a very large autoregressive language model (GPT-3) with 175 billion parameters
Applying the model to tasks in a few-shot setting without any gradient updates or fine-tuning, using only text-based prompts and demonstrations
problems: Improving few-shot learning in language models
Reducing the need for task-specific fine-tuning and large labeled datasets
Achieving strong performance on a wide range of NLP tasks with minimal task-specific adaptation

results.value: 175 billion

results.metric: Human indistinguishability of news articles (human evaluation)
results.value: Humans have difficulty distinguishing GPT-3 generated articles from human-written ones

novelty_claims: Demonstrates that scaling up language models to unprecedented size (175B parameters) enables strong few-shot learning without fine-tuning
Shows that a single large language model can perform competitively on a wide range of NLP tasks using only text-based prompts and a few examples
Establishes that few-shot learning performance improves substantially with model scale, sometimes rivaling state-of-the-art fine-tuned models
Reveals that GPT-3 can generate highly realistic text, including news articles that are difficult for humans to distinguish from real ones
